title: The Meta-Analysis Ecosystem Model: Evidence Velocity, Effect Decay, and a Future-Proof Index Across 501 Cochrane Reviews
author: Mahmood Ahmad
body: Does the magnitude of early meta-analytic effects systematically decay as new evidence accumulates across the Cochrane Library? The Meta-Analysis Ecosystem Model analysed 3,651 trajectories from 501 Cochrane reviews with at least five studies each, using cumulative fixed-effect meta-analysis on binary outcomes over a median span of 16 years. MEM computes evidence velocity, effect decay ratios, and trajectory stability, integrating them with publication bias scores into a composite Future-Proof Index scored zero to 100. Among 2,817 analyses, the median decay ratio was 0.66 (IQR 0.32 to 1.23), with 67.3 percent showing shrinkage and 25.1 percent exhibiting sign reversals, though only 3.4 percent were clinically meaningful. Velocity, decay, and bias showed negligible correlations with all absolute Pearson r below 0.02, confirming these are independent dimensions. Two-thirds of pooled effects shrank over time, underscoring the danger of treating early meta-analytic results as definitive. A limitation is that FPI component weights were expert-assigned rather than empirically optimised from calibration data.

sentences.text: Does the magnitude of early meta-analytic effects systematically decay as new evidence accumulates across the Cochrane Library?

The Meta-Analysis Ecosystem Model analysed 3,651 trajectories from 501 Cochrane reviews with at least five studies each, using cumulative fixed-effect meta-analysis on binary outcomes over a median span of 16 years.
MEM computes evidence velocity, effect decay ratios, and trajectory stability, integrating them with publication bias scores into a composite Future-Proof Index scored zero to 100.

Among 2,817 analyses, the median decay ratio was 0.66 (IQR 0.32 to 1.23), with 67.3 percent showing shrinkage and 25.1 percent exhibiting sign reversals, though only 3.4 percent were clinically meaningful.
Velocity, decay, and bias showed negligible correlations with all absolute Pearson r below 0.02, confirming these are independent dimensions.
Two-thirds of pooled effects shrank over time, underscoring the danger of treating early meta-analytic results as definitive.
A limitation is that FPI component weights were expert-assigned rather than empirically optimised from calibration data.
